title: Coulomb's Law
targetIntuitions: Charge comes in two kinds, plus and minus, and objects charge up by transferring electrons
Charged objects exert a force across empty space: opposite signs attract, same signs repel
Force weakens with distance as one over r squared, not just r
Force scales linearly with each charge
Coulomb's Law F = k q1 q2 / r squared ties these together, with force along the line and equal-and-opposite
When several charges act on one, the net force is the vector sum (superposition)
learningArc: What is charge -> charging by transfer -> force without contact (sign) -> distance squared -> charge linear -> assemble and name the law -> units and a real calculation -> direction and superposition -> mastery

steps.title: Electricity is everywhere

visual.description: A balloon clinging to a few strands of hair, with small spark marks, to introduce static electricity from everyday life.

steps.body: Have you ever pulled a sweater over your head and heard a crackle, or felt a tiny zap from a doorknob? That crackle is electric charge on the move. Charge is something that certain tiny particles carry, and it is behind lightning, static cling, and every spark. In this lesson we build, from zero, the one law that predicts the force between charges.

steps.title: Two kinds of charge

visual.description: A simple atom: a core of protons (+) with a couple of electrons (-) around it on an orbit.

steps.body: Everything is built from atoms. Each atom has a dense core holding protons, which carry positive charge (+), surrounded by lighter electrons, which carry negative charge (-). When the numbers match, the charges balance and the atom is neutral. Charge comes in just these two kinds, plus and minus. That is the whole cast of characters.

steps.title: Charging by transfer

visual.description: Two objects A and B with movable electron dots. A Rub button transfers 3 electrons from A to B and updates each object's charge badge.

steps.prompt: Rub the two objects together. Electrons jump from A to B. Afterward, what is the overall charge on object A?

feedback.correct: Exactly. A gave away negative electrons, so what is left has more protons than electrons. Losing electrons makes an object positive.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: Losing negative electrons leaves extra positive protons behind, so the object becomes positive, not negative.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: Once electrons move away, the two objects are no longer balanced. Look at the badge on A after rubbing.

steps.explanation: Charging happens by moving electrons. The object that loses electrons becomes positive; the object that gains them becomes negative. No charge is created, it is only shared. This only works between different materials, like wool and rubber: one grips electrons harder than the other. Rub two identical objects and nothing transfers.

steps.title: What a force needs

visual.description: Two charged objects a small gap apart with a question mark, asking what is required for an electric force.

steps.prompt: Two objects sit a small gap apart. What is required for them to push or pull on each other electrically?

choices.text: One must be made of metal

feedback.correct: Right. The electric force needs charge on both objects, and it reaches across a gap with no contact.

wrong.text: Think about the balloon and hair from the start. They never touched, and neither was metal. The one thing that mattered was charge.

steps.explanation: Charged objects pull or push each other across empty space. No contact, no metal, no wires required, only charge on both.

steps.title: Force across empty space

visual.description: A positive and a negative charge with arrows pointing toward each other across the gap.

steps.body: From here on we deal with charged particles. Two charges exert a force on each other straight across the space between them, with nothing in between. The force can be a pull (attraction) or a push (repulsion). Two things decide which it is, and how strong: the signs of the charges, and the distance between them. Let's pin down the signs first.

steps.title: Opposite charges attract

visual.description: A fixed positive charge and a draggable negative charge. An attraction arrow appears and grows as the gap shrinks.

steps.prompt: Drag the negative charge toward the positive one. What does the force do?

choices.text: It pulls them together, and grows as they get closer

feedback.correct: Right. Opposite signs attract, and the pull gets stronger as the gap shrinks.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: Watch the arrow as you drag closer. It points toward the red charge, meaning a pull, not a push.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: The arrow appears across the empty gap, before anything touches. Drag closer and look again.

steps.explanation: A positive and a negative charge attract. The force arrow points from each charge toward the other, and it strengthens as they approach.

steps.title: Tap the attracting pair

visual.description: Two tappable diagrams: a same-sign pair with outward arrows, and an opposite-sign pair with inward arrows.

choices.text: The same-sign pair, plus and plus
The opposite-sign pair, plus and minus

feedback.correct: Yes. Opposite signs attract, so their arrows point inward. Same signs repel, so their arrows point outward.

wrong.text: Look at the arrow directions. Inward-pointing arrows mean a pull. Which pair has them?

steps.explanation: The behavior comes from the product of the signs. Opposite signs (a negative product) attract; same signs (a positive product) repel.

steps.title: Distance weakens the force

visual.description: Two positive charges with an outward repulsion arrow. Dragging one farther shrinks the arrow.

steps.prompt: These two positive charges repel. Drag one farther away. What happens to the force?

feedback.correct: Yes. More distance means a weaker force. Exactly how much weaker is the next thing to nail down.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: Drag outward and watch the arrow. It shrinks as the gap grows.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (C)
wrong.text: Drag much farther and compare the arrow length. It clearly changes.

steps.explanation: Distance and force are inversely related: farther apart means weaker. The exact rule is sharper than most people expect, which we measure next.

steps.title: Aim the force

visual.description: A negative charge with a draggable force arrow, and a positive charge off to the left as the target direction.

steps.prompt: Drag the arrow to show which way the force pushes the negative charge.

feedback.correct: Spot on. The negative charge is pulled straight toward the positive one.

wrong.text: The charges have opposite signs, so they attract. Point the arrow from the negative charge toward the positive one.

steps.explanation: Attraction points each charge toward the other, along the line that joins them. The force on the negative charge aims right at the positive charge.

steps.title: How much weaker?

visual.description: A charge pair and a small table with rows r, 2r, 3r and unknown force values.

steps.body: We know the force weakens with distance. But by how much? If you double the distance, is the force cut in half? To a third? Less? Let's measure it and fill in this table.

steps.title: Triple the distance

visual.description: A distance slider from 1r to 3r with a live force readout starting at 1.0.

steps.prompt: Slide to three times the distance (3r). What does the force become?

feedback.correct: One ninth, not one third. Tripling the distance divides the force by nine.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (A)
wrong.text: Read the force at exactly 3r. It drops well below one third, all the way to one ninth.
Move the slider to 3r and read the value carefully before choosing.

steps.explanation: The force follows one over r squared. Tripling r means dividing by 3 squared, which is 9. Squaring makes distance powerful.

steps.title: Predict at 4r

visual.description: Same slider setup. Choices appear first; a confirmation slider to 4r is revealed only after a correct prediction.

steps.prompt: Before you slide, predict the force at four times the distance (4r).

feedback.correct: 1/16. You predicted it before checking, which means the inverse-square rule has clicked.

wrong.text: Apply the rule you just found: the force goes as one over r squared. What is one over 4 squared?

steps.explanation: At n times the distance, the force is one over n squared of the original. At 4r that is 1/16. This same inverse-square law governs gravity and the brightness of light.

steps.title: Why squared

visual.description: A point charge with expanding concentric spheres, labeled that area grows as r squared.

steps.body: Why squared, and not just distance? Picture the influence of a charge spreading outward in every direction, like paint coating a balloon. As the balloon grows, the same paint has to cover a sphere whose area grows as r squared, so the coating thins as one over r squared. Light and gravity fade with distance for the very same reason.

steps.title: Force at 5r

visual.description: Two rows: a close pair labeled F at r, and a far pair labeled with a question mark at 5r.

steps.prompt: The force is F at distance r. Using the inverse-square rule, what is the force at 5r? Enter it as a fraction of F.

feedback.correct: Correct. One over 5 squared is 1/25 of F.

wrong.text: Use one over n squared. Here n is 5, so compute one over 5 squared.

steps.explanation: Five times the distance means one over 5 squared, which is 1/25. The force falls off fast.

steps.title: The other dial

visual.description: Side-by-side panels contrasting the distance dial (squared) with the charge dial (linear).

steps.body: Distance is one dial on the force, and it is squared, so it hits hard. Charge is the other dial. We have nailed down distance. Now let's see what changing the amount of charge does. This one turns out to be simpler.

steps.title: Double the charge

visual.description: A charge-magnitude slider from 1x to 4x with a live force readout, at fixed distance.

steps.prompt: Double the amount of charge on q2. What happens to the force?

feedback.correct: Exactly. Charge enters in a straight line: double the charge, double the force.

wrong.label: Wrong feedback (B)
wrong.text: Quadrupling would mean charge is squared, like distance. Slide to 2x and read the force: is it 2 or 4?
Move the slider to exactly 2x and read the force before choosing.

steps.explanation: Force is proportional to each charge directly, to the first power. Double either charge and the force doubles. No squaring here.

steps.title: Both charges change

visual.description: One charge enlarged to 3x and the other to 2x at fixed distance, asking for the force factor.

steps.prompt: You triple one charge and double the other, keeping the distance fixed. The force becomes how many times as strong?

feedback.correct: Right. Tripling multiplies by 3, doubling by 2, and 3 times 2 is 6.

wrong.text: Each charge multiplies the force on its own. Multiply the two factors together: 3 times 2.

steps.explanation: Because force depends on q1 times q2, the two factors simply multiply: 3 times 2 gives 6 times the force.

steps.title: Build the law

visual.description: A fraction with empty numerator and denominator slots, and a tray of pieces q1, q2, r squared, and r to place.

steps.prompt: You've found all the pieces. Charge makes the force stronger, and greater distance makes it much weaker. Assemble Coulomb's Law by placing each piece where it belongs.

feedback.correct: That's it. The charges multiply on top, and r squared sits on the bottom.

wrong.text: Charge makes the force bigger, so q1 and q2 go on top. Distance makes it weaker as r squared, so r squared goes on the bottom.

steps.explanation: The charges on top give the linear scaling you found, and the distance squared on the bottom gives the inverse-square falloff.

steps.title: Coulomb's Law

visual.description: The finished formula F = k q1 q2 / r squared with the constant k below it.

steps.body: You just built Coulomb's Law, named for Charles-Augustin de Coulomb, who measured these forces in the 1780s with a delicate twisting balance. The q1 q2 on top is the linear charge scaling you found. The r squared on the bottom is the inverse-square law you measured. The k is a constant that makes the units work out, about 8.99 x 10^9. The formula only names what you already discovered.

steps.title: Units that matter

visual.description: A short list of units: coulombs for charge, the electron charge, and newtons for force.

steps.body: A quick word on units. Charge is measured in coulombs, written C. One electron carries only 1.6 x 10^-19 C, so a single coulomb is an enormous pile of charge. Put coulombs for the charges and meters for the distance into the law, and the force comes out in newtons, written N, the standard unit of force.

steps.title: A real calculation

visual.description: A 3 C charge and a 4 C charge two meters apart, asking for the force in newtons.

steps.prompt: A 3 C charge and a 4 C charge sit 2 m apart. Using k about 9 x 10^9, what is the force in newtons? Scientific notation is fine.

feedback.correct: Yes, about 2.7 x 10^10 N. Putting real numbers into the law gives tens of billions of newtons, which shows how strong the electric force is.

wrong.text: Plug the numbers into the law: k times q1 times q2 divided by r squared. Here q1 times q2 is 12 and r squared is 4, so the force is 9e9 times 12 divided by 4.

steps.explanation: Using the full law, the force is k times q1 times q2 divided by r squared. That is 9e9 times 3 times 4 divided by 2 squared, or 9e9 times 12 divided by 4, which equals 2.7 x 10^10 N. Real charges are usually millionths of a coulomb, which is why everyday forces are far smaller.

steps.title: Direction and reaction

visual.description: Two charges with equal and opposite arrows along the line joining them.

steps.body: One more detail: direction. The force always lies along the straight line joining the two charges, either toward each other or away. And the two charges always feel equal and opposite forces, the same size in opposite directions, no matter how different their charges are. That is Newton's third law showing up in electricity.

steps.title: Two factors at once

visual.description: A before/after comparison: double both charges and double the distance.

steps.prompt: Using the law: if you double the distance AND double both charges, does the force increase, decrease, or stay the same?

feedback.correct: Stays the same. Doubling both charges multiplies by 4, doubling distance divides by 4, and the two cancel.

wrong.text: Do it in two parts. Doubling both charges multiplies force by 2 times 2 = 4. Doubling distance divides by 2 squared = 4. Combine them.

steps.explanation: New force = k (2q1)(2q2) / (2r) squared = k 4 q1 q2 / 4 r squared = k q1 q2 / r squared, identical to the original. The two dials happen to cancel.

steps.title: Many charges at once

visual.description: Two fixed charges each pulling a test charge, with the two arrows adding to a single net arrow.

steps.body: So far we have looked at only two charges at a time. In the real world a charge feels many others at once. The trick is simple: work out the force from each other charge on its own, then add those forces together like arrows, tip to tail. The single result is the net force. This adding-up is called superposition.

steps.title: Combine two forces

visual.description: A board with a fixed + on the left and a fixed - on the right, plus a draggable + test charge showing a live net-force arrow.

steps.prompt: The board has a + charge on the left and a - charge on the right. Drag the + test charge close to the left + charge. Which way does the net force push it?

choices.text: To the right, away from the + and toward the -
To the left, toward the + charge
There is no net force anywhere

feedback.correct: Right. The left + pushes it away to the right, and the right - pulls it toward itself, also to the right, so the two add up.

wrong.text: Near the left + charge, that charge pushes the test charge strongly away. The - charge on the right also pulls it that way. Add the two arrows.

steps.explanation: Each fixed charge acts on its own. Near the left +, both the push from the + and the pull from the - point the same way, so the net force is large and to the right.

steps.title: Find the balance point

visual.description: Two fixed + charges and a + test charge locked to the line between them; the learner slides it to where the net force is zero.

steps.prompt: Now both fixed charges are positive. Slide the + test charge to the single spot where their two pushes cancel, so the net force drops to zero. Then check it.

feedback.correct: You found it. Right in the middle the two equal pushes are exactly opposite, so they cancel and the net force is zero.

wrong.text: Watch the net-force number as you slide. Move toward the side whose push is winning, until the two balance near the middle.

steps.explanation: With two equal charges, the forces cancel at the midpoint. Closer to either charge, that one wins and pushes you back, which makes the midpoint a stable balance point.

steps.title: You built the law

visual.description: The formula F = k q1 q2 / r squared with a summary of sign, distance, charge, and superposition.

steps.body: That's Coulomb's Law, built from scratch. Sign sets the direction: opposite attracts, same repels. Distance is squared, so the force weakens fast as one over r squared. Charge is linear, so doubling a charge doubles the force. And when many charges act at once, you add their forces as vectors. One formula captures all of it, and you derived every piece by hand.
